note: Deterministic, dependency-free readability rubric. Metrics are computed with light comment/string stripping and language-agnostic heuristics, not a full parser, so treat the numbers as an approximation. A tree-sitter version (v1) will replace the heuristics with AST-based metrics and add per-function length. Every metric here is 'lower is better': the per-category sub-score is 10 at or below 'good', 0 at or above 'bad', and linear between.
scoring: Each metric yields one per-category sub-score in [0, 10]. The composite is the PLAIN UNWEIGHTED MEAN of those sub-scores. There are no weights on purpose: with no human-scored data we do not claim one category matters more than another, and an unweighted mean makes that assumption explicit rather than hiding a taste choice inside weights. The per-category sub-scores are stored next to the composite in every metrics.json, so a weighted or fitted variant can be derived LATER without regenerating any solution.
calibration: The good/bad bands below are v0 heuristics chosen by hand, not fitted. To move off taste, fit both the bands and any per-category weights against a human-scored corpus of readability ratings, then report the held-out correlation (e.g. Spearman) so the score's agreement with human judgement is a measured number, not a claim. Candidate corpora (verified to exist): Buse & Weimer, 'Learning a Metric for Code Readability', IEEE TSE 2010 (100 Java snippets rated by 120 people); Scalabrino et al., 'A Comprehensive Model for Code Readability', J. Software: Evolution and Process 2018 (Java snippets with human ratings).
